introSection: Mindbender was a steel indoor roller coaster at Galaxyland in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Mindbender opened on 20 December 1985. It ultimately closed in July 2021.

In January 2023, it was announced that Mindbender would never reopen. The trains were sold to Indiana Beach for use on All American Triple Loop. A scale model of the coaster was donated to the National Roller Coaster Museum.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: Mindbender has a compact layout reminiscent of Quimera, the other Schwarzkopf triple looper, and several attractions intertwine with Mindbender — most notably Galaxyland's 4-D Theater and Galaxy Orbiter.

sections.title: Colour scheme
sections.content: Red track and white supports. The Vertical loops featured yellow supports.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The final car on one of the 12-person trains faces backwards.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content:
On 14 June 1986, a wheel assembly failure led to the train's fourth car to derail and crash into a concrete pillar. Four riders were ejected from the car and fell onto the concrete floor. Three of the four were killed in the accident, while the fourth one was critically injured and rushed to the hospital. 19 other people were also rushed to the hospital with minor conditions.

Following this accident, the ride underwent modifications and reopened in January 1987. The train length was reduced by one car, reducing the rider capacity from 16 to 12 people. Anti-Rollback features were also added, as were two wheel assemblies to the trains. The lap bar restraints were kept, but seat belts, and headrests were added.

sections.title: External links
sections.content:
* Mindbender on the parks website
 * A site housing a picture of the crash.

fullText:
Mindbender was a steel indoor roller coaster at Galaxyland in Edmonton, Alberta, Canada.

Mindbender opened on 20 December 1985. It ultimately closed in July 2021.

In January 2023, it was announced that Mindbender would never reopen. The trains were sold to Indiana Beach for use on All American Triple Loop. A scale model of the coaster was donated to the National Roller Coaster Museum.

Mindbender has a compact layout reminiscent of Quimera, the other Schwarzkopf triple looper, and several attractions intertwine with Mindbender — most notably Galaxyland's 4-D Theater and Galaxy Orbiter.

Red track and white supports. The Vertical loops featured yellow supports.

The final car on one of the 12-person trains faces backwards.

On 14 June 1986, a wheel assembly failure led to the train's fourth car to derail and crash into a concrete pillar. Four riders were ejected from the car and fell onto the concrete floor. Three of the four were killed in the accident, while the fourth one was critically injured and rushed to the hospital. 19 other people were also rushed to the hospital with minor conditions.

Following this accident, the ride underwent modifications and reopened in January 1987. The train length was reduced by one car, reducing the rider capacity from 16 to 12 people. Anti-Rollback features were also added, as were two wheel assemblies to the trains. The lap bar restraints were kept, but seat belts, and headrests were added.




 * Mindbender on the parks website
 * A site housing a picture of the crash.
